title: Saja Elshareif -Hiring Manager Interview - Andi | Technical Managing Editor
meeting_title: Saja Elshareif -Hiring Manager Interview - Andi | Technical Managing Editor

transcript.text: This meeting is being recorded.
I'm so sorry I'm late.
Hi, good morning.
No problem.
No issue at all.
Thank you for having me.
Thanks me on the call today.
Yeah, thanks for scheduling some time.
How are you doing?
Good, thank you.
How about yourself?
I'm good.
Today, we jumped right in, but here we are.
Where are you based out of?
I am just outside of Chicago, Illinois.
Oh, amazing.
So you're even earlier than I am.
You're really jumping right in.
Yes.
All right.
Well, thanks for scheduling something for 8.15 in the morning.
Of course.
I'm Andi.
I lead customer operations for GrowthX.
I would love to get started by just giving you a little bit of background on myself, the role, kind of how we're thinking about it within the context of GrowthX.
And then you can let me know or give me a little bit of background on yourself.
I'll ask you some questions, and then I'll try and leave like five, probably five minutes at the end since I joined a little late.
But yes.
that
And I will leave time for you to ask me questions.
Yeah, so to give you a little context on myself, I've been at GrowthX for about two months, two and a half months.

Customer operations means a lot of things at a lot of companies, but at GrowthX, it means that I'm thinking about kind of the process, inputs, all of the pieces that come together to generate the deliverables for our clients.
So to me, like our SOWs and contractual obligations for the clients.
So some of that's like team management, thinking about capacity planning, things like that.
And some of that is on the other end, we have an internal platform that we've built that we're generating all of our content off of.
So this role actually kind of sits right between those two things, too.
And there are two really main areas of focus that we think about.
The

The first is like a bit of a project manager and, you know, we have the word technical at the front of the technical managing editor role today because we are using our internal platform and it's six weeks old.
It's been like live and in the wild for almost as long as I've been at GrowthX, which is, again, not very long.
So it's like a really powerful system.
It's leveraging different AI tools throughout kind of a content creation process from outline generation to deep research and then like to the actual like article generation and refinement pieces.
But throughout that process, you know, we want a human in the loop and we want somebody who can also help like troubleshoot.
It's not perfect yet.
There are a lot of moving pieces.
And so somebody who's willing.

To give feedback to the engineering team, help refine kind of what each workflow looks like that's generating content, and know how to communicate kind of like when something needs to be improved and how it can be better.
So that is the, that's the like technical and project management piece.
The other side of it is, of course, like once we generate deliverables, making sure the clients have, are getting kind of what they need.
So there is a bit of a client facing aspect of that project management, and just telling clients where we are in the process, status updates, things like that.
This role works kind of as the hub, I would say, of a pod that serves each client.
So today that pod looks like a technical managing editor as like the project manager, and also a little bit like literally the editor of content.
Before it goes to the client, you're making sure that it matches like tone and style and is actually what the client's looking for.
Within the running of the workflows, we have content managers who they're managed separately.

So this isn't a management role, but they're actually like pressing play on the workflows, generating first drafts, kind of like refining look and feel as much as they can before it goes to you for a second round of edits.

And then we have a director who is really the strategic lead for the pods and the person who thinks about kind of long term, where the client's going on their kind of like journey, how we best serve them, what we want to deliver, and kind of planning the road ahead in terms of the client relationship.
So that's what the pod looks like.
That's what the role looks like.
like.
We have kind of a lot of time to explain that, but do you have any questions?
No, it all makes sense to explain that perfectly, actually.

Yeah, I'm really excited because it overlaps with exactly what I was doing and like an informal title, but it's actually exactly what I was doing, kind of balancing between client-facing communication and feedback, but also making sure deliverables are getting there on time, communicating, you know, what that timeline looks like and so on.
So this is, and of course, the editing and the writing, so that it makes sense.
Perfect.
Well, why don't you give me a bit of an overview on your latest role and also kind of your background?
So basically, I started out in linguistics.
That's where I have my master's, and I just really have a love for language, writing, storytelling, and just putting together great content.
That's kind of where it all started.
I moved on to teaching college courses, and then I actually went, you know, my husband.
And then had his own business as an accountant, and I just added on my own business with my own clients.
And I built my clientele over like, you know, 10 years plus, basically editing, writing, content creating, business planning and solutions.
So I was all across the board.
From there, I worked, I met a CEO, she's a neurologist of a physician-led company.
And basically what she did is mentor physicians that were working internationally, and she helps them, you know, get into residency training here in the U.S.
So she brought me on very early on.
was a startup.
And I was brought on as the director of writing.
Can you still hear me?
I think your screen is frozen.
Okay.
Just that the picture is frozen.
That's okay.
That's okay.
And so basically, I, you know, I handle
Everything from internal operations, getting out those early templates, intake forms, editing and documenting everything internally, and managing and leading her team of physicians.
But I also faced the clients and did all the editing and writing for the clients that we actually had.
So it was really just kind of balancing both of these two different, you know, worlds that we just kind of went through at GrowthX.
And then most recently, you know, you know, the physician decided that she's going to wind down the company.
So she kind of stopped taking new clients and just finished up any projects that we had already started.
And that allowed me to just move on to AI and writing for AI.
So since then, I have been working kind of as a prompt engineer, but also reviewing other prompt writers' work, model responses, assessing...
Other new AI models are being efficient and effective at what they're supposed to do.
And I was brought on to Snorkel as a subject matter expert in linguistics.
And basically, my job was to stump the model, try to write these prompts that are based on textual evidence and resources and citations.
But basically, I was trying to find the loopholes in the AI that they're building.
So that we could identify those as, you know, weak spots that we can improve.
Amazing.
Well, tell me a little bit more about your work at Out...
I mean, like, I just want to know everything about your last role.
So Outlier and Snorkel.
I guess, talk to me about, you mentioned, like, kind of working as a prompt engineer.
What does that look like?
And I guess, like, what have you learned and what surprised you in doing that work?
Absolutely.
Absolutely.
So...
So it's been, you know, just around over a year mark right now.
And basically what I did, I started with Outlier.
And Outlier has their own clients that have developed AI models.
And we, you know, basically were brought on to test these models to assess them based on a rubric.
So, for example, the rubric might say, okay, you're going to work for the next two months.
You're going to work on image to text.
So we're evaluating how well this bot can describe this image in text, okay?
So my job is to write high-quality prompts with constraints, with, you know, specific requests in order to evaluate whether the AI model understands what is depicted in the image.
That's just one example.
So I did audio-to-text, I did video-to-text, I did text-to-text.
And, you know, some projects I was, it's like 50.
Terms that you're going with the bot.
Sometimes it's just a couple of terms.
And basically, you're just evaluating, you know, every step of the way.
So I was quickly promoted to reviewer.

So then I began reviewing other prompt engineers' prompts, giving them constructive feedback about what was working, what was not working, how to improve those prompts, so that we were testing the models, you know, as best as possible.
So that's outlier.
And Snorkel, like I said, it was first, it was linguistics-based.
And then they quickly, you know, promoted me to be able to work on any field, basically, that I had experience in.
So I was doing English, assessing, basically, AI models that were early in their stage of development, and some of those that were much more advanced.
And really trying to stump those advanced models was a huge challenge for me.
But it was exciting.
It was a lot of fun, because they're able to learn so quickly.

And it has the memory built in and everything else, but you're really trying to get through those loopholes to say, hey, I found something, you know, this is where it's getting stumped over and over again.
And this is what we need to do.
So I work closely with the engineering.
I work closely with, you know, those that were just reviewing the project managers that were reviewing the progress.
And in turn, they would take that feedback and give it to the client.
What I learned overall is how important, you know, input is in producing, you know, high-quality output.
So I learned that, you know, you find basically a pattern of how to either stump the model, but in turn, you also find out how to get high-quality output from AI.
And that's through, you know, adding in the constraints, the specifics needed, clarity in your question and in your, you know, request.
Um, and just being able to communicate, um, in a way with accuracy so that you're getting the output that you experienced.
Um, that, yeah, that makes a lot of sense.
I, I like that.
I mean, that's also, I think, what we're learning.
Um, and sometimes it is surprising how much, like, how much context it needs.
Absolutely.
Yeah.
Um, cool.
Well, I, so let's talk a little bit about your, um, client management experience.
know these, these have been like really a evaluation roles more recently.
Um, but, um, talk to me maybe a little bit about your early experience, earlier experience.
And you mentioned like balancing like that communication feedback and then working on client deliverables.
Absolutely.
resident success is the name of the RS is the name of the company that I worked with, um, very closely with the CEO and COO.
And basically, um, eventually.
I became a partner in that startup.
And what really I had to do there was, you know, because it's a startup, of course, you wear many hats, right?
You are helping with the social media, and we're helping, you know, with the business plans, and we're helping with the writing and editing.
But essentially, my job was to take on all the writing and any editing that we got.
So I handled all clients.
So that did amount to about 75 plus deadlines per month.
And those are, you know, personal statements, CVs, presentations, just all across the board, technical writing.
And I had, I was the main point of contact for each and every client.
And in fact, I was also the point of contact for new leads that were coming in, anybody that had questions about the writing, or what we did, or so on.
I held webinars.
webinars.

was in presentations, both promotional and paid for these physicians that were asking about what we did and, you know, what writing we can help them produce for their applications and for their programs that they were entering.
So I was the client facing person and one of the leaders that was most recognizable, I would say, in the team.
The other thing that I did was I started taking on management roles, not just in, you know, just the operations of the company, but also more specifically in social media.
So I, they did not have a social media team prior to, you know, just a couple years ago.
And basically I, I interviewed and put together a team and I taught them all to use AI in order to automate the social media posts and content to generate that, to get a calendar going and so on.
So really, I think that, you know, in this company.

I ended up at the end, or more recently, in just around January to March, I ended up being the AI expert, so somebody who's teaching everybody the growth hacks and how to implement AI, so that we're automating everything that we've been doing.

Instead of editing, you know, five posts for the week, here's the input that you can put into ChatGPT or whatever we were using, and here is the output that would need to be edited, and then you can send it to me to look over, and then we can build this calendar to go out.
So I ended up doing a lot of teaching about AI and managing all of the content, editing that content before it was finally posted.
Okay, yeah.
Sorry, I'm turning back on my camera.
That's okay.
I'm the camera off.
I just feel like it's so unfair.
Okay, that's amazing.
I feel like you have a really good, like, balance of, and I'm so interested in, like, your more recent work and then your client-facing work.
Is there...
So like, which side of that coin do you prefer?
And I don't think there's the right answer.
I'm just curious about it.
Like in terms of the research and the learning and the AI prompting, and then the, you know, you did a lot of like client facing work and deliverables on that side.
Yes.
I think that it is really a blend of both.
I'm a people person.
I love to work, you know, with people.
I love to join and attend meetings to be a leader to empower, you know, who I'm leading to teach new tools.
So I do like the blend of both.
I'm very comfortable managing a team, giving feedback, communicating regularly.
But I'm also very interested and enjoy writing and editing as well.
So the content side.
So I really do think that it's, it's this perfect blend of both.
And I'm really like excited that growthx kind of is asking for both of those things.
So that is, that is really exciting for me.
Awesome.
I'm curious, like, are there other kinds of roles that you're looking for right now?

Or like, what's, you know, what is, I know, I just, a bit of a different take on the same question that I just asked, but I'm curious, like, you know, where you want to go with what your goals are in terms of like the evolution of your career.
I think leadership.
mean, I would like to be really managing projects.
I would like to oversee, you know, large projects at a larger scale, and to help them develop.
I'm definitely not somebody that sits back and, you know, just wants to be in that comfortable desk job where everything's the same every single day.
I like to keep moving.
I like to contribute in thinking and to be just in an innovative atmosphere where things are changing and evolving and improving.
So I do like to be part of those conversations.
So I think that leadership, you know, and I say leadership, because I do, you know, I think there's a difference between being like a motivational type of manager or leader, you know, where you say.
Hey, good job, you got this, we can do it, which is great.
But I do really like to empower, you know, whoever I'm working with, my team.
And what I mean by that is I like to be able to step in and say, hey, you know, I noticed that you've been missing, you know, a couple deadlines, and that's unlike you, here's what you can do.
And then let's check back in a week and see how that's working.
So I like to be able to teach, you know, to give off some tools and to give that mentorship so that we're able to get back on track and work together.
Awesome.
Cool.
Well, those are all the questions I have for you.
And we have eight minutes left.
So what can I answer for you?
I'm just, I'm wondering, you know, I know that everything is kind of new and shuffling around.
I saw that, you know, and I actually just saw it later on, I saw that you had a post before, and I think maybe you were on LinkedIn, and perhaps you were transitioning jobs or something.
And you said something about said something about

Here I think I have it, about kind of changing, you know, sometimes that your role changes, here it is, that, you know, some companies look for somebody specifically that's almost been in the same job, you know, a very strict box for like 15 years, and that's kind of what they're looking for.

And that's all that they're seeing, is, how is growthx, and, you know, comparison, and what they look for, and, you know, and I think I have, you know, a lot to bring to the table, I have a lot of technical skills, I have, you know, strong set of soft skills, and sometimes it's difficult to really communicate that forward, so it's difficult to have people see the value, and all of that put together, so how do you see that fit at growthx?

Yeah, that was, I was like job searching, and I've been job searching for a little while, and I wrote that post, because I'd been like applying, I had come from a startup, I'd been applying at like big companies.
Because I was like, look, I've like done these seven things, like you want me to do one of those things, I could totally just focus on one of those things.
So similar to you, you know, like, picking up different pieces.
And in my interview process, I was just finding that, like, bigger companies are like, yes, well, we have, we just need a cog in this wheel.
And like, if you've, if you're a cog and like a spoke, we are not interested in that.
Yeah.
And I think, growthx, like, it's a startup, it is like, it's under a year old.
And I, it, there's so much that needs to be built, so much process that needs to be put in place.
Like, and so much opportunity for anybody that is curious, and, like, wants to take the lead or shape the future of something.
There's a lot of opportunity to do that.
So yesterday, for example, we have like our
So we call our client-facing org the delivery team.
We had a delivery team all hands, and we've been using Airtable forever in terms of like client, kind of to communicate with clients, like the status of projects.
But Airtable is a little bit like technical.
It's not just an Excel spreadsheet.
It's like, you know, there's like a lot of things it can do, and sometimes that power is overwhelming to clients.
And so we have gotten to the point where we're also finding that clients didn't want to use the Airtable because they were like, I've never used Airtable, and I don't know how to read this.
So one of our MEs, who's in the same position that you're applying for, she built like an app interface.
Like Airtable has like a new AI like app offering.
She was like, okay, you don't want to read the Airtable?
Great.
I'm going to build an app that just shows you what you need to see.
You don't have to see all the confusing parts of this project management.
And she presented it to the 40 people in our all hands yesterday, because I was like, this is a really cool process.
Like, thank you for solving this problem.
Everybody, if you want to do this, like, this is how Carrie built this.
So I think there's a lot of opportunity to shape, as I said, shape, like, our process, help, like, set a standard for what good looks like.
One of our cultural values is a best hire mentality.
And that's just, like, showing up for our clients and saying, if you, if our client was giving me the paycheck directly, like, how would I want to make sure that I was justifying that paycheck?
Like, you know, they're paying us to be their marketing department, in some cases.
And so I think that, like, that creates a lot of space to, to just do what you think is.
Fathom's to deliver for them.
Rather than saying, like, we've done this 700 million times, you just do the 701st one, you know?
Right.
And so, like, that, it's a lot more fun.
Sometimes it makes it more stressful, and I will not lie to you, but it's also, like, it's not boring.
No, I get that.
I know what it's like to work at a startup, and I have used Airtable.
I used it in the last four months or so.
was using it over the last four months for it at Snorkel.
So I know that that can be confusing when it's client-facing.
I just wanted to ask you as well, what metrics kind of matter or are being measured, you know, in my potential role?
How is, like, success nature there?
Yeah.
Yeah.
So we are still putting in place, like, metrics both internally and externally.
It's actually, like, a focus.
Yeah.
Right now, it's something that's top of mind for me.
So our contracts are really like output based in terms of the, like, this is how many articles we're delivering per week or, you know, things like that.
And so at a bare minimum, it's just like, what is the output of this team?
we meeting client deliverables?
Because that's kind of like your, that's what this person is supposed to help ensure.
So just meeting client deliverables is the bare minimum thing we're measuring now.
The way that I think that this role will evolve is once we get to the point where we can, like, formally start to measure, like, CSAT, I think this person is going to be really key to that.
And then in terms of the technical side, and this is much softer, and I don't really know how we're going to measure this yet, but because...
We are engaging so much in developing the workflows in our own internal platform, which eventually may become its own SaaS offering.
We want, like, there is like a UX component and like a feedback component to this.
Again, I don't have a, like, we don't have a way to measure that today.
changing, But, like, I mean, that's also like an opportunity area of as we grow, if we become bigger, like, we probably will need more, like, product feedback and strengthening that feedback loop.
And so this person will help just build up that muscle just in their day-to-day of saying, hey, this didn't work.
Hey, I think this is broken and reporting bugs and thinking about the product while still meeting the client deliverables.
That makes sense.
And then the last question, is there, like, ladder in place for growth, right?
Now, or is it still kind of fluid?
It's pretty, it's pretty amorphous, I'll say.
I mean, we haven't even been around for a full year.
We do have, for now, like, a bonus structure in place where, like, people that are doing good work for a short period of time or something, like, we have some incentives, I would say, in place.
And the pod structure, in terms of, like, what a team of people serving a client looks like and what those roles are, has changed, like, twice this year.
So I think, like, yes, the goal has always been to allow, especially this project manager layer, the opportunity to, like, be, step up in a more strategic role.
I don't, like, we haven't done.
But I also think, like, given the level of growth that we're seeing, it will only make sense to, like, enable that ladder.
Makes sense.
Okay, I think that was everything.
And then I remember that Mariela, she mentioned, like, a take-home assessment or something.
And that would be after this conversation?
Yes, that's after this conversation.
It's basically, I would say, in terms of, like, priming you for it, it really shouldn't take more than an hour and a half.

And given what you've told me about, like, prompting and things like that, you know, we're looking for meeting, like, how you're leveraging prompts to achieve, like, match kind of what a client is already doing in terms of their own applications.
what, what, what, what,
Okay, that makes sense.
Okay, and then any advice or questions or anything else that you have, like anything about my experience or, you know, that makes you hesitate or question or anything at all?
No, I've really, really enjoyed this conversation.
And yeah, I think there's like two more steps in this process, and I will probably speak to you again in the panel portion of it.
Okay, sounds great.
Thank you so much.
Great.
Thank you, Saja.
Have a good day.
You too.
Bye.

## Meeting Purpose

[Hiring manager interview for Technical Managing Editor role at GrowthX with candidate Saja Elshareif.](https://fathom.video/share/oNhNeK6TPr8wsp5rhum3JiNs2LnfE4HM?tab=summary&timestamp=0.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [Role involves project management, client communication, and content editing using GrowthX's AI-powered platform](https://fathom.video/share/oNhNeK6TPr8wsp5rhum3JiNs2LnfE4HM?tab=summary&timestamp=240.0)
  - [Saja has extensive experience in content creation, client management, and AI prompt engineering](https://fathom.video/share/oNhNeK6TPr8wsp5rhum3JiNs2LnfE4HM?tab=summary&timestamp=580.0)
  - [GrowthX offers opportunities for innovation and shaping processes in a startup environment](https://fathom.video/share/oNhNeK6TPr8wsp5rhum3JiNs2LnfE4HM?tab=summary&timestamp=1430.0)
  - [Success metrics focus on meeting client deliverables, with potential for expanding into CSAT and product feedback](https://fathom.video/share/oNhNeK6TPr8wsp5rhum3JiNs2LnfE4HM?tab=summary&timestamp=1671.0)

## Topics

### Role Overview

  - [Technical Managing Editor acts as hub of client-serving pod](https://fathom.video/share/oNhNeK6TPr8wsp5rhum3JiNs2LnfE4HM?tab=summary&timestamp=464.0)
  - [Responsibilities include:](https://fathom.video/share/oNhNeK6TPr8wsp5rhum3JiNs2LnfE4HM?tab=summary&timestamp=420.0)
      - [Project management using GrowthX's 6-week-old internal AI platform](https://fathom.video/share/oNhNeK6TPr8wsp5rhum3JiNs2LnfE4HM?tab=summary&timestamp=360.0)

      - [Editing content for tone, style, and client expectations](https://fathom.video/share/oNhNeK6TPr8wsp5rhum3JiNs2LnfE4HM?tab=summary&timestamp=480.0)
  - [Works with content managers (separate management) and strategic director](https://fathom.video/share/oNhNeK6TPr8wsp5rhum3JiNs2LnfE4HM?tab=summary&timestamp=511.0)

### Candidate Background

  - [Master's in linguistics with 10+ years of editing, writing, and content creation experience](https://fathom.video/share/oNhNeK6TPr8wsp5rhum3JiNs2LnfE4HM?tab=summary&timestamp=580.0)
  - [Recent roles:](https://fathom.video/share/oNhNeK6TPr8wsp5rhum3JiNs2LnfE4HM?tab=summary&timestamp=580.0)

          - [Managed 75+ client deadlines monthly](https://fathom.video/share/oNhNeK6TPr8wsp5rhum3JiNs2LnfE4HM?tab=summary&timestamp=1049.0)

      - [AI prompt engineer and reviewer at Outlier and Snorkel](https://fathom.video/share/oNhNeK6TPr8wsp5rhum3JiNs2LnfE4HM?tab=summary&timestamp=779.0)

          - [Identified model weaknesses and provided improvement feedback](https://fathom.video/share/oNhNeK6TPr8wsp5rhum3JiNs2LnfE4HM?tab=summary&timestamp=910.0)

### AI Experience and Insights

  - [Promoted to reviewer role, providing feedback to other prompt engineers](https://fathom.video/share/oNhNeK6TPr8wsp5rhum3JiNs2LnfE4HM?tab=summary&timestamp=850.0)

  - [Developed strategies for both challenging and optimizing AI models](https://fathom.video/share/oNhNeK6TPr8wsp5rhum3JiNs2LnfE4HM?tab=summary&timestamp=895.0)

### Client Management Experience

### Career Goals and Preferences
